generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components
instance CycloidalGearActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Mechanical amplification component using cycloidal gear transmission paired with a permanent magnet synchronous motor for controlled motion of the first vertical robot axis"
]

instance PermanentMagnetSynchronousMotor : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first vertical robot axis with real-time control over position, speed, or acceleration via Siemens SINUMERIK 840d sl platform"
]

instance SiemensSinumerik840dSlController : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Real-time control system platform implementing position, speed, and acceleration control with enforced safety constraints within [-90°, 90°] angular range"
]

// C3: Physical sensing components
instance MotorEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Motor encoder mounted on the permanent magnet synchronous motor for angular position measurement"
    DTDFVocab:producedData MotorPositionData
]

instance GearboxEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Secondary encoder positioned behind the cycloidal gearbox for high-precision spatial measurement of mechanical amplification"
    DTDFVocab:producedData MechanicalPositionData
]

// C4: Physical-to-virtual interaction
instance MotorPositionData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotorEncoder
]

instance MechanicalPositionData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom GearboxEncoder
]

instance FrictionTorqueData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotorCurrentSensor
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom TorqueSensor
]

instance AngularVelocityData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotorEncoder
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance MotionAccuracyDashboardModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Visualization interface model combining real-time joint friction metrics, motion trajectories, and control error profiles for production supervision"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLIBDModel
]

instance FrictionCompensationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Behavioral model implementing state machines for friction anomaly detection and actuator parameter adjustment"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulinkModel
]

instance DigitalShadowReferenceModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "UML-based class diagram defining one-way data flow from physical sensors to digital shadow aggregation layer for contextualized motion accuracy analysis"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MQTTProtocol
]

instance MotionTrajectoryData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated data traces of robot motion trajectories for accuracy validation and comparison"
    DTDFVocab:fromData MechanicalPositionData
    DTDFVocab:fromData AngularVelocityData
]

instance FrictionMetricsData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Contextualized data traces of joint friction measurements for control optimization"
    DTDFVocab:fromData FrictionTorqueData
]

instance ControlErrorData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Real-time error profiles between desired and actual motion parameters for accuracy assessment"
    DTDFVocab:fromData MotorPositionData
    DTDFVocab:fromData MechanicalPositionData
]

// C11: Enablers
instance SysMLIBDModel : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML Interaction Block Diagrams for modeling component interfaces, connectors, and behavior of the robot's functional architecture"
    DTDFVocab:enables MotionAccuracyDashboardModel
]

instance SimulinkModel : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink environment for validating behavioral models including friction dynamics and control algorithms"
    DTDFVocab:enables FrictionCompensationModel
]

instance MQTTProtocol : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Message Queuing Telemetry Transport protocol for low-latency streaming of sensor data from physical robot to digital shadow"
    DTDFVocab:enables DigitalShadowReferenceModel
]

instance MBSEFramework : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Model-Based Systems Engineering framework utilizing SysML and RFLP methodology for hierarchical system decomposition"
    DTDFVocab:enables MotionAccuracyDashboardModel
    DTDFVocab:enables FrictionCompensationModel
]

// Additional missing instances needed for FrictionTorqueData references
instance MotorCurrentSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Current sensor measuring motor input current for friction torque estimation"
    DTDFVocab:producedData FrictionTorqueData
]

instance TorqueSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Direct torque sensor measuring mechanical output torque for friction analysis"
    DTDFVocab:producedData FrictionTorqueData
]

// C6: Services
instance MotionAccuracyDashboardService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Real-time visualization interface displaying joint friction metrics, motion trajectories, and control error profiles for production supervision"
    DTDFVocab:provides DashboardVisualizationInsight
]

instance FrictionBasedControlOptimizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Autonomous service dynamically adjusting actuator parameters to compensate for detected joint friction anomalies"
    DTDFVocab:provides FrictionCompensationAction
    DTDFVocab:provides AccuracyImprovementInsight
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance DashboardVisualizationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Contextualized data traces and aggregated insights from motion accuracy metrics displayed in real-time dashboard for operational decision-making"
]

instance FrictionCompensationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Autonomous adjustment of motor torque and gearbox ratios to compensate for detected friction anomalies"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance AccuracyImprovementInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Performance metrics and recommendations derived from friction compensation analysis showing improved motion accuracy"
]

// C17: Environment
instance RobotAxisEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains PermanentMagnetSynchronousMotor
    base:contains CycloidalGearActuator
    base:contains MotorEncoder
    base:contains GearboxEncoder
    base:contains SiemensSinumerik840dSlController
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system operates in a **real-time, data-driven** environment, where live-data linking ensures synchronized state representation between the physical and digital components. While the document does not specify exact synchronization frequencies or granularity requirements, it emphasizes the necessity of **automated one-way data flow** for passive digital shadows (e.g., contextual data aggregation and abstraction) and the integration of **active components** (e.g., digital twins) to process and interpret incoming data. This implies a requirement for **low-latency synchronization** to maintain accurate digital twin fidelity, particularly in manufacturing contexts where real-time operational dependencies exist (e.g., robotics control)."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The **Digital Twin constellation** is defined through a **UML class-based reference model** (as depicted in Fig. 1) that outlines key concepts for **digital shadows** in manufacturing. While the document does not explicitly describe a **Digital Twin constellation architecture**, it implies an **orchestration framework** where:
  - **Component integration** is achieved via **contextual data traces** and their aggregation (e.g., via digital shadows).
  - **System-wide coordination** is facilitated by **active components** in Digital Twins (e.g., software systems interpreting data) to enable autonomous operations.
  - **Resource management** is implicit in the distinction between **passive digital shadows** (one-way data flow) and **active Digital Twins** (bidirectional interaction), suggesting a modular, stakeholder-integrated approach (e.g., via MBSE methodologies like SysML or ADLs). The absence of explicit patterns (e.g., microservices, edge computing) implies a **domain-specific abstraction layer** for manufacturing, where coordination is tied to **state machines** (e.g., MontiArc Automaton) or simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink) for behavior modeling."
]

    base:desc "
  **Not in Document** (No explicit lifecycle stages or representation types for Digital Twins are described in the provided text. The document focuses on modeling methods, digital shadows, and their distinctions rather than DT lifecycle phases or governance approaches.).

  **Note:** The provided text mentions **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SMArDT) for system development, which could imply iterative phases (requirements → architecture → implementation), but these are not directly linked to Digital Twin lifecycle stages in the document.
 "
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
Fidelity and validity in the Digital Twin are grounded in **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, and **SMArDT**, which systematically decompose the system into abstraction layers (requirements → functional → logical → physical). The **Digital Shadow** acts as a foundational passive data layer, aggregating contextual traces without autonomous processing, while the **Digital Twin** (as an active component) interprets these traces via **Siemens SINUMERIK 840d sl** or similar tools (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) for behavioral validation.

Uncertainty sources are not explicitly quantified, but the system’s **safety constraints** (e.g., angular limits) and **redundant sensing** (dual encoders) imply inherent error mitigation. No iterative validation methods (e.g., **model-based simulation loops**, **anomaly detection**) or quality assurance processes (e.g., ISO/IEC standards) are detailed. The documents emphasize **active components** (e.g., control systems) to process sensor data but do not specify formal validation protocols or error correction mechanisms.
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
  The **digital twin’s technical connection** leverages the **MQTT protocol** for communication between the physical system (Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller) and its digital counterpart. While the documents do not specify network topology, security protocols, or redundancy mechanisms explicitly, the following details are inferred from the context:

  - **Communication Protocol**: **MQTT** (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport), optimized for low-latency, event-driven industrial IoT communication.
  - **Industrial Controller**: The **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl** acts as the primary physical controller, interfacing with the digital twin via MQTT for real-time data exchange.
  - **Bandwidth/Latency**: Not specified, but MQTT’s lightweight nature suggests minimal latency requirements typical in industrial automation (e.g., <100ms for critical control loops).
  - **Security**: No explicit mention of encryption (e.g., TLS) or authentication (e.g., MQTT user IDs/passwords) in the documents, but industrial standards (e.g., MQTT-SN for secure variants) may apply implicitly.
  - **Reliability**: No redundancy mechanisms (e.g., failover clusters) are documented, but the **one-way data flow** (digital shadow) implies passive reliability—data is processed centrally without bidirectional dependencies.
"
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
  The **digital twin hosting and deployment** infrastructure is not explicitly detailed in the documents but can be inferred from the provided context:

  - **Hosting Platform**: The system appears to be **on-premise or edge-deployed**, given the reliance on the **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl** controller (a hardware-based industrial automation system) and the use of **MathWorks MATLAB Simulink** for executable simulation models.
  - **Computational Requirements**: The **MATLAB Simulink** environment suggests moderate computational resources (e.g., a PC with sufficient RAM/CPU for real-time simulation loops), but exact specifications (e.g., CPU cores, memory) are not provided.
  - **Storage**: No explicit mention of storage capacity or data retention policies, but the aggregation of **time-series sensor data** implies persistent storage for historical analysis.
  - **Scalability**: Not addressed in the documents; the system may scale horizontally (e.g., distributed MQTT brokers) or vertically (e.g., upgrading hardware) based on industrial practices.
  - **Deployment Models**: Likely follows a **monolithic or microservices** approach, given the integration of **Simulink models** (a standalone simulation tool) with the digital shadow framework.
  - **Availability/Reliability**: The **dashboard** component implies a user-facing interface, but no guarantees on uptime (e.g., 99.9% availability) or redundancy (e.g., failover servers) are documented.
  - **Key Tools**:
    - **MathWorks MATLAB Simulink** for executable simulation models (e.g., friction modeling in the first axis).
    - **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl** as the primary physical controller interfacing with the digital twin.
"
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system integrates with **external systems** via standardized protocols and enterprise frameworks. Key dependencies include:
  - **MQTT** for real-time data streaming between digital shadows and active twins.
  - **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)** for interoperability with enterprise IT systems.
  - **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl** controller integration for robotics automation.
  These integrations ensure seamless data exchange and system-wide coordination, though the document does not specify detailed integration patterns (e.g., API endpoints, message formats) or scope of dependencies beyond these technologies."
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
  **Compliance and Interoperability:**
  The Digital Twin/Digital Shadow system adheres to industry-specific standards:
  - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs):** **AADL**, **ACME**, **MontiArc**, and **SysML IBDs** for component/connector modeling.
  - **Asset Administration Shells (AAS):** Centralized representation of physical assets via standardized interfaces (e.g., **OPC UA** implied by AAS frameworks).
  - **Data Protocols:** **MQTT** for lightweight, event-driven communication between physical and digital entities.
  - **Simulation Tools:** **MathWorks Simulink** for dynamic behavior modeling.

  **Certification and Compliance Frameworks:**
  While the documents reference **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., **RFLP**) and **ADLs**, explicit compliance with **ISO/IEC 23247** (Digital Twin standards) or **OPC Foundation** specifications is **not mentioned**. Interoperability is assumed through **AAS** and ADL integration, but no formal compliance audits or certification bodies are detailed.
  "
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
    **Cybersecurity Protocols:**
    The Digital Twin system leverages the **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework (as referenced in [Document 50](https://www.plattform-i40.de/IP/Redaktion/EN/Downloads/Publikation/Details_of_the_Asset_Administration_Shell_Part1_V2.html)), which enables standardized cybersecurity integration through modularized security components. The AAS adheres to **ISO/OSI layer-based security principles**, ensuring secure data exchange via **MQTT protocol** (as documented in [Document 52](https://thingsboard.io)), which supports lightweight, efficient communication with built-in authentication and encryption mechanisms.

    **Access Control Mechanisms:**
    The system employs **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for defining security policies and access control rules within the Digital Twin environment. This ensures role-based authorization (e.g., read/write permissions for different stakeholders) and enforces **conformance relations** (as described in [Document 41](https://logos-verlag.de/2024/konersmann-et-al-towards-reference-models-with-conformance-relations-for-structure)), ensuring only authorized entities can interact with critical assets.

    **Physical Safety Considerations:**
    The **RAMI 4.0 reference architecture** (as outlined in [Document 48](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/8500000) and [Document 49](https://www.din.de/en/industry-4-0/rami40)), particularly the **Physical Safety Cell layer**, restricts the operational range of robotic systems (e.g., MABI Max-100 robotic arm, as per [Document 44](https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p)) to prevent collisions and ensure deterministic fail-safe behavior under extreme conditions. The system integrates **friction modeling** (as described in [Document 45](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-46216-4_36)) to predict and mitigate dynamic safety risks in motion control.

    **Threat Mitigation Strategies:**
    The Digital Twin employs **digital shadows** (as discussed in [Document 42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0167799X2500032X)) to simulate real-time operational states, enabling proactive threat detection via anomaly analysis. The **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** methodology (as detailed in [Document 43](https://ieeeexplore.ieee.org/document/10345000)) ensures consistency between physical and digital models, reducing vulnerabilities by validating system integrity through structured conformance checks.

    **Fail-Safe Mechanisms:**
    The system incorporates **deterministic fail-safe mechanisms** via the **Functional Mockup Interface (FMI)** (as referenced in [Document 51](https://linkoe.se/8th-international-modelica-conference)), enabling automated shutdowns or emergency stops in case of critical deviations. The **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** further supports **safety-critical system considerations** by providing standardized interfaces for integrating safety-related components (e.g., emergency stop buttons, pressure sensors) into the Digital Twin lifecycle.
  "
]
